[2026-07-04 18:18:39 INFO] Process design expert: **Process design expert<->Team leader on : ProcessGeneration, turn 0**

[You're a team leader. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
Your main responsibilities will include guiding the process design expert through process generation, process refinement, and so on.
Here is a new customer's task: Module 2: Early Treatment
This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The
majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital
and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or
comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should
be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10).
Module 2 Recommended Practices 
2.1 Glucose management
2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately
2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first
random glucose value is > 11 mmol/L
2.2 Body temperature
2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs
every 4 hours for first 48 hours
For temperature > 37.5 C:
• initiate temperature-reducing measures
• investigate potential infection
• initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required
2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should
be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention
assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed:
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high-
risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and
initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated)
as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset,
followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each
patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends
that:
o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor
stroke clinicc
o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic
pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur
at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be
given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke
prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of
accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure
equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of
the location of initial presentation
o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from
symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a
specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient
clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial
presentation
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without
high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of
antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as
possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation,
followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention
servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1
month of symptom onset
2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or
crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech
difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission
to hospital
2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on
an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on
antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain
imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage:
• ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81–
325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of
81 mg/day
• clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day
• extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with
ASA 160 mg first)
2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as
for patients who fail swallowing screening
2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE,
National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.
aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high
risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery
atherosclerosis with > 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event.
bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging
cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and
vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests.
dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus.
eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or
arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.
Module 2 Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as
possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED
• Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA
Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department..
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

<process>
  <activity role='ED clinician' action='identify patient as having suspected TIA or minor nondisabling stroke and initiate standardized priority protocol' objects='suspected stroke patient' id='activity1'/>
  <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway1'>
    <branch id='branch1'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='measure random blood glucose' objects='blood glucose value' id='activity2'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway1'>
        <branch condition='hypoglycemia is present' id='branch2'>
          <activity role='ED clinician' action='correct immediately' objects='hypoglycemia' id='activity3'/>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway2'>
            <branch condition='first random glucose value > 11 mmol/L' id='branch3'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='repeat blood glucose measurement' objects='blood glucose value' id='activity4'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='first random glucose value <= 11 mmol/L' id='branch4'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='continue glucose management pathway' objects='glucose results' id='activity5'/>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='hypoglycemia is not present' id='branch5'>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway3'>
            <branch condition='first random glucose value > 11 mmol/L' id='branch6'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='repeat blood glucose measurement' objects='blood glucose value' id='activity6'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='first random glucose value <= 11 mmol/L' id='branch7'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='continue glucose management pathway' objects='glucose results' id='activity7'/>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch8'>
      <activity role='nursing staff' action='evaluate temperature as part of routine vital signs every 4 hours for first 48 hours' objects='body temperature and vital signs' id='activity8'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway4'>
        <branch condition='temperature > 37.5 C' id='branch9'>
          <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway2'>
            <branch id='branch10'>
              <activity role='nursing staff' action='initiate temperature-reducing measures' objects='temperature management measures' id='activity9'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch11'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='investigate potential infection' objects='possible infection source' id='activity10'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch12'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='initiate antipyretic therapy as required' objects='antipyretic therapy' id='activity11'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch13'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='initiate antimicrobial therapy as required' objects='antimicrobial therapy' id='activity12'/>
            </branch>
          </parallelGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='temperature <= 37.5 C' id='branch14'>
          <activity role='nursing staff' action='continue routine temperature monitoring every 4 hours for first 48 hours' objects='temperature chart' id='activity13'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch15'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='perform cross-continuum secondary prevention assessment and therapies for all patients whether admitted or discharged' objects='Modules 5 and 10 prevention assessments and therapies' id='activity14'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch16'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='assess TIA or minor stroke risk features, symptom onset timing, current antiplatelet use, swallowing status, and fluctuating or crescendo symptoms' objects='clinical presentation and history' id='activity15'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway5'>
        <branch condition='TIA with high-risk features or minor stroke' id='branch17'>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway6'>
            <branch condition='medical attention sought after 48 hours from symptom onset' id='branch18'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='refer within 24 hours of initial presentation for evaluation at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention services' objects='urgent referral' id='activity16'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='medical attention sought within 48 hours from symptom onset' id='branch19'>
              <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway7'>
                <branch condition='specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic is immediately accessible without risky delay' id='branch20'>
                  <activity role='stroke clinic team' action='provide immediate care at specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic' objects='rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation' id='activity17'/>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='delay to specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic poses risk to patient health' id='branch21'>
                  <activity role='ED team' action='perform evaluation at appropriately resourced ED and consider inpatient evaluation and management for stroke prevention' objects='ED evaluation and admission consideration' id='activity18'/>
                </branch>
              </exclusiveGateway>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
          <activity role='ED clinician' action='obtain brain CT scan as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset' objects='brain CT scan' id='activity19'/>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway8'>
            <branch condition='fluctuating or crescendo hemibody motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty is present' id='branch22'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='evaluate in the ED and consider admission to hospital' objects='high-risk TIA patient' id='activity20'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='fluctuating or crescendo hemibody motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty is absent' id='branch23'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='continue ambulatory or clinic-based stroke prevention management' objects='patient care plan' id='activity21'/>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='TIA without high-risk features' id='branch24'>
          <activity role='ED clinician' action='obtain brain CT scan as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation' objects='brain CT scan' id='activity22'/>
          <activity role='ED clinician' action='refer to outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention services for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1 month of symptom onset' objects='outpatient stroke-prevention referral' id='activity23'/>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway9'>
            <branch condition='fluctuating or crescendo hemibody motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty is present' id='branch25'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='evaluate in the ED and consider admission to hospital' objects='high-risk TIA patient' id='activity24'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='fluctuating or crescendo hemibody motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty is absent' id='branch26'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='continue outpatient management planning' objects='patient care plan' id='activity25'/>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway10'>
        <branch condition='brain imaging excludes intracranial hemorrhage and patient is not on an antiplatelet agent and antiplatelet therapy is not contraindicated' id='branch27'>
          <inclusiveGateway id='inclusivegateway1'>
            <branch condition='ASA selected' id='branch28'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='start antiplatelet therapy immediately with ASA 160 mg loading dose followed by enteric coated ASA 81 to 325 mg daily, with most patients maintained on 81 mg/day' objects='ASA regimen' id='activity26'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='clopidogrel selected' id='branch29'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='start antiplatelet therapy immediately with clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose followed by 75 mg/day' objects='clopidogrel regimen' id='activity27'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='extended-release dipyridamole plus ASA selected' id='branch30'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='start antiplatelet therapy immediately with ASA 160 mg loading dose then extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid' objects='extended-release dipyridamole plus ASA regimen' id='activity28'/>
            </branch>
          </inclusiveGateway>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway11'>
            <branch condition='patient fails swallowing screening or requires non-oral administration' id='branch31'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='offer rectal or gastric ASA as necessary' objects='rectal ASA or gastric ASA' id='activity29'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='patient passes swallowing screening and oral administration is suitable' id='branch32'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='administer oral antiplatelet regimen' objects='selected oral antiplatelet therapy' id='activity30'/>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='intracranial hemorrhage is not excluded or patient is already on an antiplatelet agent or antiplatelet therapy is contraindicated' id='branch33'>
          <activity role='ED clinician' action='withhold immediate new antiplatelet initiation and tailor further stroke-prevention treatment to patient status' objects='individualized stroke-prevention plan' id='activity31'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>
  <activity role='ED team' action='complete disposition to discharge with urgent clinic follow-up or admit to hospital or stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to the ED as indicated' objects='final care disposition' id='activity32'/>
</process>

| Parameter | Value |
| --- | --- |
| **task_prompt** | Module 2: Early Treatment This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10). Module 2 Recommended Practices  2.1 Glucose management 2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately 2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first random glucose value is &gt; 11 mmol/L 2.2 Body temperature 2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs every 4 hours for first 48 hours For temperature &gt; 37.5 C: • initiate temperature-reducing measures • investigate potential infection • initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required 2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies 2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10) 2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke 2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed: • OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high- risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset, followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends that: o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinicc o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of the location of initial presentation o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial presentation • OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation, followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1 month of symptom onset 2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission to hospital 2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage: • ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81– 325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of 81 mg/day • clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day • extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with ASA 160 mg first) 2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as for patients who fail swallowing screening 2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE, National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack. aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery atherosclerosis with &gt; 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event. bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests. dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus. eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk. The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel. Module 2 Implementation Considerations General considerations • Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED • Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department. |

| **task_prompt** | Module 2: Early Treatment This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10). Module 2 Recommended Practices  2.1 Glucose management 2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately 2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first random glucose value is &gt; 11 mmol/L 2.2 Body temperature 2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs every 4 hours for first 48 hours For temperature &gt; 37.5 C: • initiate temperature-reducing measures • investigate potential infection • initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required 2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies 2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10) 2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke 2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed: • OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high- risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset, followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends that: o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinicc o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of the location of initial presentation o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial presentation • OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation, followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1 month of symptom onset 2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission to hospital 2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage: • ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81– 325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of 81 mg/day • clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day • extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with ASA 160 mg first) 2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as for patients who fail swallowing screening 2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE, National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack. aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery atherosclerosis with &gt; 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event. bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests. dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus. eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk. The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel. Module 2 Implementation Considerations General considerations • Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED • Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department. |

[2026-07-04 18:18:39 INFO] Team leader: **[Start Chat]**

[You're a process design expert. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
You are familiar with exclusive gateway, parallel gateway and inclusive gateway in BPMN. Your main responsibilities include process generation, process refinement, process review and so on, guided by team leader.
You model the process of a clinical practice guideline to a clinical pathway.
Here is a new customer's task: Module 2: Early Treatment
This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The
majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital
and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or
comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should
be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10).
Module 2 Recommended Practices 
2.1 Glucose management
2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately
2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first
random glucose value is > 11 mmol/L
2.2 Body temperature
2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs
every 4 hours for first 48 hours
For temperature > 37.5 C:
• initiate temperature-reducing measures
• investigate potential infection
• initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required
2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should
be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention
assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed:
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high-
risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and
initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated)
as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset,
followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each
patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends
that:
o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor
stroke clinicc
o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic
pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur
at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be
given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke
prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of
accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure
equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of
the location of initial presentation
o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from
symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a
specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient
clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial
presentation
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without
high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of
antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as
possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation,
followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention
servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1
month of symptom onset
2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or
crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech
difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission
to hospital
2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on
an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on
antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain
imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage:
• ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81–
325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of
81 mg/day
• clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day
• extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with
ASA 160 mg first)
2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as
for patients who fail swallowing screening
2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE,
National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.
aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high
risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery
atherosclerosis with > 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event.
bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging
cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and
vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests.
dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus.
eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or
arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.
Module 2 Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as
possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED
• Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA
Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department..
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

task: Module 2: Early Treatment
This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The
majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital
and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or
comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should
be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10).
Module 2 Recommended Practices 
2.1 Glucose management
2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately
2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first
random glucose value is > 11 mmol/L
2.2 Body temperature
2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs
every 4 hours for first 48 hours
For temperature > 37.5 C:
• initiate temperature-reducing measures
• investigate potential infection
• initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required
2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should
be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention
assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed:
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high-
risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and
initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated)
as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset,
followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each
patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends
that:
o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor
stroke clinicc
o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic
pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur
at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be
given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke
prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of
accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure
equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of
the location of initial presentation
o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from
symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a
specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient
clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial
presentation
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without
high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of
antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as
possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation,
followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention
servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1
month of symptom onset
2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or
crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech
difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission
to hospital
2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on
an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on
antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain
imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage:
• ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81–
325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of
81 mg/day
• clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day
• extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with
ASA 160 mg first)
2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as
for patients who fail swallowing screening
2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE,
National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.
aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high
risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery
atherosclerosis with > 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event.
bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging
cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and
vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests.
dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus.
eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or
arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.
Module 2 Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as
possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED
• Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA
Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department.
process text:
<process>
  <activity role='ED clinician' action='identify patient as having suspected TIA or minor nondisabling stroke and initiate standardized priority protocol' objects='suspected stroke patient' id='activity1'/>
  <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway1'>
    <branch id='branch1'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='measure random blood glucose' objects='blood glucose value' id='activity2'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway1'>
        <branch condition='hypoglycemia is present' id='branch2'>
          <activity role='ED clinician' action='correct immediately' objects='hypoglycemia' id='activity3'/>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway2'>
            <branch condition='first random glucose value > 11 mmol/L' id='branch3'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='repeat blood glucose measurement' objects='blood glucose value' id='activity4'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='first random glucose value <= 11 mmol/L' id='branch4'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='continue glucose management pathway' objects='glucose results' id='activity5'/>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='hypoglycemia is not present' id='branch5'>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway3'>
            <branch condition='first random glucose value > 11 mmol/L' id='branch6'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='repeat blood glucose measurement' objects='blood glucose value' id='activity6'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='first random glucose value <= 11 mmol/L' id='branch7'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='continue glucose management pathway' objects='glucose results' id='activity7'/>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch8'>
      <activity role='nursing staff' action='evaluate temperature as part of routine vital signs every 4 hours for first 48 hours' objects='body temperature and vital signs' id='activity8'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway4'>
        <branch condition='temperature > 37.5 C' id='branch9'>
          <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway2'>
            <branch id='branch10'>
              <activity role='nursing staff' action='initiate temperature-reducing measures' objects='temperature management measures' id='activity9'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch11'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='investigate potential infection' objects='possible infection source' id='activity10'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch12'>

            </branch>
            <branch id='branch13'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='initiate antimicrobial therapy as required' objects='antimicrobial therapy' id='activity12'/>
            </branch>
          </parallelGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='temperature <= 37.5 C' id='branch14'>
          <activity role='nursing staff' action='continue routine temperature monitoring every 4 hours for first 48 hours' objects='temperature chart' id='activity13'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch15'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='perform cross-continuum secondary prevention assessment and therapies for all patients whether admitted or discharged' objects='Modules 5 and 10 prevention assessments and therapies' id='activity14'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch16'>
      <activity role='ED clinician' action='assess TIA or minor stroke risk features, symptom onset timing, current antiplatelet use, swallowing status, and fluctuating or crescendo symptoms' objects='clinical presentation and history' id='activity15'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway5'>
        <branch condition='TIA with high-risk features or minor stroke' id='branch17'>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway6'>
            <branch condition='medical attention sought after 48 hours from symptom onset' id='branch18'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='refer within 24 hours of initial presentation for evaluation at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention services' objects='urgent referral' id='activity16'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='medical attention sought within 48 hours from symptom onset' id='branch19'>
              <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway7'>
                <branch condition='specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic is immediately accessible without risky delay' id='branch20'>
                  <activity role='stroke clinic team' action='provide immediate care at specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic' objects='rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation' id='activity17'/>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='delay to specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic poses risk to patient health' id='branch21'>
                  <activity role='ED team' action='perform evaluation at appropriately resourced ED and consider inpatient evaluation and management for stroke prevention' objects='ED evaluation and admission consideration' id='activity18'/>
                </branch>
              </exclusiveGateway>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
          <activity role='ED clinician' action='obtain brain CT scan as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset' objects='brain CT scan' id='activity19'/>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway8'>
            <branch condition='fluctuating or crescendo hemibody motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty is present' id='branch22'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='evaluate in the ED and consider admission to hospital' objects='high-risk TIA patient' id='activity20'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='fluctuating or crescendo hemibody motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty is absent' id='branch23'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='continue ambulatory or clinic-based stroke prevention management' objects='patient care plan' id='activity21'/>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='TIA without high-risk features' id='branch24'>
          <activity role='ED clinician' action='obtain brain CT scan as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation' objects='brain CT scan' id='activity22'/>
          <activity role='ED clinician' action='refer to outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention services for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1 month of symptom onset' objects='outpatient stroke-prevention referral' id='activity23'/>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway9'>
            <branch condition='fluctuating or crescendo hemibody motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty is present' id='branch25'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='evaluate in the ED and consider admission to hospital' objects='high-risk TIA patient' id='activity24'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='fluctuating or crescendo hemibody motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty is absent' id='branch26'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='continue outpatient management planning' objects='patient care plan' id='activity25'/>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway10'>
        <branch condition='brain imaging excludes intracranial hemorrhage and patient is not on an antiplatelet agent and antiplatelet therapy is not contraindicated' id='branch27'>
          <inclusiveGateway id='inclusivegateway1'>
            <branch condition='ASA selected' id='branch28'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='start antiplatelet therapy immediately with ASA 160 mg loading dose followed by enteric coated ASA 81 to 325 mg daily, with most patients maintained on 81 mg/day' objects='ASA regimen' id='activity26'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='clopidogrel selected' id='branch29'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='start antiplatelet therapy immediately with clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose followed by 75 mg/day' objects='clopidogrel regimen' id='activity27'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='extended-release dipyridamole plus ASA selected' id='branch30'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='start antiplatelet therapy immediately with ASA 160 mg loading dose then extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid' objects='extended-release dipyridamole plus ASA regimen' id='activity28'/>
            </branch>
          </inclusiveGateway>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway11'>
            <branch condition='patient fails swallowing screening or requires non-oral administration' id='branch31'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='offer rectal or gastric ASA as necessary' objects='rectal ASA or gastric ASA' id='activity29'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='patient passes swallowing screening and oral administration is suitable' id='branch32'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='administer oral antiplatelet regimen' objects='selected oral antiplatelet therapy' id='activity30'/>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='intracranial hemorrhage is not excluded or patient is already on an antiplatelet agent or antiplatelet therapy is contraindicated' id='branch33'>
          <activity role='ED clinician' action='withhold immediate new antiplatelet initiation and tailor further stroke-prevention treatment to patient status' objects='individualized stroke-prevention plan' id='activity31'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>
  <activity role='ED team' action='complete disposition to discharge with urgent clinic follow-up or admit to hospital or stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to the ED as indicated' objects='final care disposition' id='activity32'/>
</process>
Check which activities in the process text can be expanded into sub-activities and add these sub-activities to the process text. Please note:
1) Only expand important activities, not all of them.
2) Ensure that the process text remains logical when adding sub-activities.
3) Add appropriate gateways when adding activities.
4) When modifying the process text, note whether the newly added activity is a sequential, exclusive, or concurrent relationship.
5) Only expand activities if it's necessary and do not create overly detailed sub-activities.
Here are some examples of expanding activities in process texts into sub-activities. You can refer to them but cannot copy them:
1. 'payment with card/cash' activity with id='activity2' can be expanded to:
   sub-activity:'Payment with card' and 'Payment with cash' 
   activity relations: exclusive
2. 'club interview id and confimation email' activity with id='activity5' can be expanded to:
   sub-activity:'Interview at the club' and 'Send confirmation email'
   activity relations: sequential
3. 'Submit the materials' activity with id='activity9' can be expanded to:
   sub-activity:'Submit interview materials' and 'Submit examination materials' 
   activity relations: parallel
After extending the activity, number the activity or gateway in addition to the original id. If the sub-activity are exclusive or parallel relationships, you need to add them in the form of gateways when modifying the process text:

When the process starts, please output '<process>'
An activity is represented as follows:
<activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
The exclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<exclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</exclusiveGateway>
The parallel gateway is represented as follows:
<parallelGateway id=''>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</parallelGateway>
The inclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<inclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</inclusiveGateway>
At the end of the process, please output '</process>'

There are a few requirements that should be noted when generating process text:
    1) role, action, objects and id in an activity represent the subject of the activity, the activity behavior, the items needed to perform in the activity and the activity id, respectively.
    2) There are at least two branches within the exclusive gateway and the parallel gateway.
    3) Activity, gateway, and branch id attributes must be unique.
    4) When the branch in the exclusive gateway has a condition, fill the condition into the condition attribute. When the branch unconditional within the exclusive gateway, the condition attribute of the branch =''. However, all branches within a exclusive gateway can only be conditional, or both unconditional.
    5) The branch in the parallel gateway has no condition attribute.
    6) Each branch in the inclusive gateway must have condition.
    7) There must be at least one activity inside the branch tag.
    8) The activity of a sequential relation need not be represented by a gateway, that is, there is no 'sequenceGateway' label.

First output which activities can be extended according to the above format (...... can be expanded to:.......) and then give the modified process text. Don't say anything else.

[2026-07-04 18:18:39 INFO] Process design reviewer: **Process design reviewer<->Team leader on : GiveSuggestion, turn 0**

[You're a team leader. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
Your main responsibilities will include guiding the process design expert through process generation, process refinement, and so on.
Here is a new customer's task: Module 3: Admission to Acute Care
This module identifies best practices for acute inpatient admissions (as described in recommendation
2.4.2 for the specific group of TIA patients who should be admitted). Module 3a provides
recommendations for acute inpatient treatment, and module 3b provides recommendations for the
prevention of secondary complications, which should be initiated in the acute care setting but also be
considered as ongoing activities. All patients should be given appropriate cross-continuum
prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10). The Ontario Stroke Network revised the
definition of a stroke unit “A geographical unit with identifiable co-located beds (eg 5A -7, 5A-8,
5A-9, 5A-10, 5A-11) that are occupied by stroke patients 75% of the time and has a dedicated
interprofessional team with expertise in stroke care with the following professionals at a minimum
nursing, physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech language pathologist”.) Ontario Stroke
Network 2015
Module 3a: Acute Care Treatment
Module 3a Recommended Practices 
3.1 Stroke units
3.1.1 Patients should be admitted to a specialized, geographically
defined hospital unit dedicated to the management of stroke patients
A geographical unit with identifiable co-located beds (eg 5A -7, 5A-
8, 5A-9, 5A-10, 5A-11) that are occupied by stroke patients 75% of
the time and has a dedicated inter-professional team with expertise in
stroke care with the following professionals at a minimum nursing,
physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech language pathologist”.
• This recommendation is in accordance with the HQO Effectiveness
of Stroke Unit Care: A Special Report, which concluded (61):
Moderate quality evidence showed that persons admitted to a stroke
unit had a significant reduction in death and the combined outcome
of death or institutionalization, and a nonsignificant reduction in
institutionalization. Low quality evidence showed that patients
admitted to a stroke unit had a significant reduction in the combined
outcome of death or dependency and length of hospital stay and a
nonsignificant reduction in the outcome of dependency
3.1.2 The core stroke unit team should consist of health care
professionals with stroke expertise in medicine, nursing, occupational
therapy, physiotherapy, speech-language pathology, social work, and
clinical nutrition (a dietitian)
3.1.3 To have the necessary stroke expertise, the health care
professionals on the core stroke unit team should be individuals who
spend the vast majority of their time treating stroke patients and
regularly complete education about stroke care
3.1.4 Patients should have access to a specialized interprofessional
team 7 days a week
3.1.5 Physiotherapy, speech-language pathology, and occupational
therapy team members should assess stroke patients within 48 hours
of admission to hospital and formulate a management plan
3.2 Early mobilization
3.2.1 All stroke patients admitted to hospital with acute stroke should
be mobilized early(between 24 hours and 48 hours of stroke onset),
unless contraindicated
Frequent out-of-bed activity in the very early time frame (within 24
hours of stroke onset) is not recommended Mobilization may be
reasonable for some patients with acute stroke in the very early time
frame and clinical judgement should be used.
3.3 Assessment
3.3.1 Clinicians should use standardized, valid assessment tools to
evaluate patients’ stroke-related impairments and functional status
3.3.2 AlphaFIM should be completed on or by day 3 after
admission(target day 3, admission day is day 1)
3.3.3 All stroke patients should be assessed for risk of developing
venous thromboembolism
3.3.4 Early mobilization and adequate hydration should be encouraged
for all acute stroke patients to help prevent venous thromboembolism
3.3.5 The nutrition and hydration status of stroke patients should be
assessed within the first 48 hours of admission using a valid screening
tool. Stroke patients with nutritional concerns, hydration deficits,
dysphagia, or other comorbidities should be referred to a dietitian.
Referral to a dietitian should be made within 7 days of admission for
recommendations and for consideration of enteral nutrition support for
patients who are unable to meet nutritional and fluid requirements
3.4 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
3.5 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
3.5.1 All patients, whether admitted to in-hospital rehabilitation or
discharged, should be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary
prevention assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; Australia, Australian Clinical Guidelines for Stroke
Management; CSBPR, Canadian Best Practices Recommendations; FIM, Functional Independence Measure; HQO, Health Quality Ontario;
OSN, Ontario Stroke Network; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.

Module 3a Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• All hospitals providing stroke/TIA care should ensure that the interprofessional stroke team uses standardized, validated
assessment tools; where possible, these tools should be in electronic format linked to the EHR
• Patients should be screened/assessed for diabetes, dental issues, cognitive impairment, and depression upon
presentation to the ED, and the results should be forwarded to the TIA clinic for follow-up
• Hospitals should ensure adequate staffing 7 days per week
Abbreviations: ED, emergency department; EHR, electronic health record; TIA, transient ischemic attack..
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

Problem: The exclusive gateway with id='exclusivegateway2' is logically incomplete and has overlapping branch conditions for mobilization timing. In particular, the branch condition 'within 24 hours from stroke onset' does not explicitly exclude contraindication, while another branch separately handles 'mobilization is contraindicated'. This creates ambiguity because a patient who is within 24 hours and also has a contraindication could fit more than one branch.

Suggested modification: Revise the branch conditions in the exclusive gateway with id='exclusivegateway2' so they are mutually exclusive. For example:
- change branch8 condition to '24 to 48 hours from stroke onset and no contraindication to mobilization'
- change branch9 condition to 'within 24 hours from stroke onset and no contraindication to mobilization'
- keep branch12 condition as 'mobilization is contraindicated'

Reason: An exclusive gateway requires only one path to be taken. The current design allows overlap between time-based conditions and contraindication status, which is not sound BPMN decision logic. Making the conditions mutually exclusive ensures clear and correct routing of the patient to exactly one mobilization pathway.

[2026-07-04 18:18:39 INFO] **[Seminar Conclusion]**:

 Problem: The exclusive gateway with id='exclusivegateway2' is logically incomplete and has overlapping branch conditions for mobilization timing. In particular, the branch condition 'within 24 hours from stroke onset' does not explicitly exclude contraindication, while another branch separately handles 'mobilization is contraindicated'. This creates ambiguity because a patient who is within 24 hours and also has a contraindication could fit more than one branch.

Suggested modification: Revise the branch conditions in the exclusive gateway with id='exclusivegateway2' so they are mutually exclusive. For example:
- change branch8 condition to '24 to 48 hours from stroke onset and no contraindication to mobilization'
- change branch9 condition to 'within 24 hours from stroke onset and no contraindication to mobilization'
- keep branch12 condition as 'mobilization is contraindicated'

Reason: An exclusive gateway requires only one path to be taken. The current design allows overlap between time-based conditions and contraindication status, which is not sound BPMN decision logic. Making the conditions mutually exclusive ensures clear and correct routing of the patient to exactly one mobilization pathway.

Stroke patients with nutritional concerns, hydration deficits,\ndysphagia, or other comorbidities should be referred to a dietitian.\nReferral to a dietitian should be made within 7 days of admission for\nrecommendations and for consideration of enteral nutrition support for\npatients who are unable to meet nutritional and fluid requirements\n3.4 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke\n3.5 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies\n3.5.1 All patients, whether admitted to in-hospital rehabilitation or\ndischarged, should be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary\nprevention assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)\nAbbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; Australia, Australian Clinical Guidelines for Stroke\nManagement; CSBPR, Canadian Best Practices Recommendations; FIM, Functional Independence Measure; HQO, Health Quality Ontario;\nOSN, Ontario Stroke Network; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.\n\nThe following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.\n\nModule 3a Implementation Considerations\nGeneral considerations\n• All hospitals providing stroke/TIA care should ensure that the interprofessional stroke team uses standardized, validated\nassessment tools; where possible, these tools should be in electronic format linked to the EHR\n• Patients should be screened/assessed for diabetes, dental issues, cognitive impairment, and depression upon\npresentation to the ED, and the results should be forwarded to the TIA clinic for follow-up\n• Hospitals should ensure adequate staffing 7 days per week\nAbbreviations: ED, emergency department; EHR, electronic health record; TIA, transient ischemic attack.', 'suggestions': "Problem: The exclusive gateway with id='exclusivegateway2' is logically incomplete and has overlapping branch conditions for mobilization timing. In particular, the branch condition 'within 24 hours from stroke onset' does not explicitly exclude contraindication, while another branch separately handles 'mobilization is contraindicated'. This creates ambiguity because a patient who is within 24 hours and also has a contraindication could fit more than one branch.\n\nSuggested modification: Revise the branch conditions in the exclusive gateway with id='exclusivegateway2' so they are mutually exclusive. For example:\n- change branch8 condition to '24 to 48 hours from stroke onset and no contraindication to mobilization'\n- change branch9 condition to 'within 24 hours from stroke onset and no contraindication to mobilization'\n- keep branch12 condition as 'mobilization is contraindicated'\n\nReason: An exclusive gateway requires only one path to be taken. The current design allows overlap between time-based conditions and contraindication status, which is not sound BPMN decision logic. | **task_prompt** | Module 3: Admission to Acute Care This module identifies best practices for acute inpatient admissions (as described in recommendation 2.4.2 for the specific group of TIA patients who should be admitted). Module 3a provides recommendations for acute inpatient treatment, and module 3b provides recommendations for the prevention of secondary complications, which should be initiated in the acute care setting but also be considered as ongoing activities. All patients should be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10). The Ontario Stroke Network revised the definition of a stroke unit “A geographical unit with identifiable co-located beds (eg 5A -7, 5A-8, 5A-9, 5A-10, 5A-11) that are occupied by stroke patients 75% of the time and has a dedicated interprofessional team with expertise in stroke care with the following professionals at a minimum nursing, physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech language pathologist”.) Ontario Stroke Network 2015 Module 3a: Acute Care Treatment Module 3a Recommended Practices  3.1 Stroke units 3.1.1 Patients should be admitted to a specialized, geographically defined hospital unit dedicated to the management of stroke patients A geographical unit with identifiable co-located beds (eg 5A -7, 5A- 8, 5A-9, 5A-10, 5A-11) that are occupied by stroke patients 75% of the time and has a dedicated inter-professional team with expertise in stroke care with the following professionals at a minimum nursing, physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech language pathologist”. • This recommendation is in accordance with the HQO Effectiveness of Stroke Unit Care: A Special Report, which concluded (61): Moderate quality evidence showed that persons admitted to a stroke unit had a significant reduction in death and the combined outcome of death or institutionalization, and a nonsignificant reduction in institutionalization. Low quality evidence showed that patients admitted to a stroke unit had a significant reduction in the combined outcome of death or dependency and length of hospital stay and a nonsignificant reduction in the outcome of dependency 3.1.2 The core stroke unit team should consist of health care professionals with stroke expertise in medicine, nursing, occupational therapy, physiotherapy, speech-language pathology, social work, and clinical nutrition (a dietitian) 3.1.3 To have the necessary stroke expertise, the health care professionals on the core stroke unit team should be individuals who spend the vast majority of their time treating stroke patients and regularly complete education about stroke care 3.1.4 Patients should have access to a specialized interprofessional team 7 days a week 3.1.5 Physiotherapy, speech-language pathology, and occupational therapy team members should assess stroke patients within 48 hours of admission to hospital and formulate a management plan 3.2 Early mobilization 3.2.1 All stroke patients admitted to hospital with acute stroke should be mobilized early(between 24 hours and 48 hours of stroke onset), unless contraindicated Frequent out-of-bed activity in the very early time frame (within 24 hours of stroke onset) is not recommended Mobilization may be reasonable for some patients with acute stroke in the very early time frame and clinical judgement should be used. 3.3 Assessment 3.3.1 Clinicians should use standardized, valid assessment tools to evaluate patients’ stroke-related impairments and functional status 3.3.2 AlphaFIM should be completed on or by day 3 after admission(target day 3, admission day is day 1) 3.3.3 All stroke patients should be assessed for risk of developing venous thromboembolism 3.3.4 Early mobilization and adequate hydration should be encouraged for all acute stroke patients to help prevent venous thromboembolism 3.3.5 The nutrition and hydration status of stroke patients should be assessed within the first 48 hours of admission using a valid screening tool. Stroke patients with nutritional concerns, hydration deficits, dysphagia, or other comorbidities should be referred to a dietitian. Referral to a dietitian should be made within 7 days of admission for recommendations and for consideration of enteral nutrition support for patients who are unable to meet nutritional and fluid requirements 3.4 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke 3.5 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies 3.5.1 All patients, whether admitted to in-hospital rehabilitation or discharged, should be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10) Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; Australia, Australian Clinical Guidelines for Stroke Management; CSBPR, Canadian Best Practices Recommendations; FIM, Functional Independence Measure; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OSN, Ontario Stroke Network; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack. The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel. Module 3a Implementation Considerations General considerations • All hospitals providing stroke/TIA care should ensure that the interprofessional stroke team uses standardized, validated assessment tools; where possible, these tools should be in electronic format linked to the EHR • Patients should be screened/assessed for diabetes, dental issues, cognitive impairment, and depression upon presentation to the ED, and the results should be forwarded to the TIA clinic for follow-up • Hospitals should ensure adequate staffing 7 days per week Abbreviations: ED, emergency department; EHR, electronic health record; TIA, transient ischemic attack. |
